verdict_reason: All three disagreements are doctrinally substantive and represent the most metaphysically loaded terms in the chapter. (1) brāhmī sthitiḥ — the explanation correctly identifies the four traditional readings (Advaita: nondual Brahman; Vishishtadvaita: Brahman-qualified-by-devotion; Gaudiya: Kṛṣṇa-consciousness; modern translators flatten or specify per their tradition) and notes 'any English rendering must choose a tradition'. The observation that 'the metaphysics of brāhmī sthiti is not engineering-translatable, and any analog must stay at the operational layer and acknowledge the scope-mismatch honestly' is the correct meta-observation for the engineering layer. (2) brahma-nirvāṇam — the explanation correctly identifies the three traditional readings (Advaita: nondual identification = mokṣa; Vishishtadvaita: cessation-plus-entry into the realm of the Supreme; Gaudiya: entry into Vaikuṇṭha) and notes the compound's force comes from joining brahma + nirvāṇa (the latter being a major Buddhist term). (3) anta-kāle 'pi — the explanation correctly identifies the surface agreement (even at the moment of death) and the divergent implications across traditions (Shankara: brief establishment at death suffices; Ramanuja and Prabhupada: qualifying condition for entry into the supreme abode). The api ('even') structural force as anti-deathbed-anxiety is correctly named. All three explanations would survive Sanskrit-grammarian + comparative-philosophy review at the highest standard. Score 10 — gold-standard treatment of the most loaded chapter-closing verse. Pass.
